question702_v2: Not applicable to me, but ADA entrances are difficult to find as far as I know.  I mean, the grand stairs facing Mathews St. are a great entrance, but if I'm in a wheelchair or have mobility issues, I'm thinking, "How on earth do I get in here?"      As far as I know the accessible entrance is on Oregon St., which shunts visitors off into a corridor that leads one past a coat closet and offices and only then into the lobby.  I know a renovation of the scale necessary to add a ramp, etc. to the Mathews St. side would be a lot, but I'd invest there if you can.      There also isn't a bus stop near there, or someone going to Krannert is going to get off at the front steps or perhaps be dropped off there.  To find that they then need to head up an UPHILL street, and then around a corner, past the parking garage, PAST A DUMPSTER, and up a labyrinthine ramp to end up in a corridor would suck.       For a university which was such a forerunner in accessibility, to have the flagship performing arts center so inaccessible to mobility-impaired people is rather embarrassing.     Even this survey has more questions about sensory-related issues, which are, of course, quite valid, than physical accessibility.      I have a relative who is disabled, and he's also a UI alum who fondly remembers running up the KCPA steps in healthier days.  I'd feel bad having to wheel him past a dumpster to get him in there, if my commencement had been there instead of Smith Memorial Hall.  For a building built in 1917, Smith has a simple small ramp to get people right in, it knocks Krannert out of the park.  Even Altgeld, which has the most hidden elevator anywhere on campus, has ground level entrances that aren't hard to find.    St, John's Chapel, at the Newman Center, has recently added a small outdoor elevator to get around their steep stairs.  I see no reason why Krannert can't do the same.      Get some people from DRES to workshop ideas, get the engineers to come up with an elevator or ramp, and get the School of Architecture to design it to fit KCPA's aesthetic, and get something on the Mathews St. side ASAP.      In the interim, get some signage out there that CLEARLY notes where the accessible entrance is, and get some ushers (stick an ad at ARC or CRCE, pushing people in wheelchairs is a good workout) to be standing on Mathews ready to assist people to get around to Oregon and in.      And, for the love of Louis Armstrong, MOVE THE DUMPSTER AWAY FROM THE ACCESSIBLE ENTRANCE, PLEASE!    Addressing invisible disabilities is a wonderful trend in accessibility these days, but if someone happens to have sensory issues AND a physical disability, the most wonderfully customized space inside one of the theatres isn't going to matter if they can't even get in the building.

question402_v2: Having grown up near CU, but not in CU - Krannert was looked at as a place that was a privilege to get tickets to see something. It was where my school district took field trips. I remember driving over when I was in college to see several performances and feeling like it was a great venue and the music and performers that had been brought in were fantastic. (B in Music Performance) It used to be a joyful place, that seemed to breathe life.    Now, I would say there is a desperate sense of superiority at certain performances (and this may just be because they are produced/dominated by a subset of people). The community Krannert represents is a community that does not tend to be very welcome to people outside the clique (and it isn't necessarily a community of higher ed). Every theater performance in town (whether Krannert, Parkland, Station) will have the same people, uncorked the same... and you will see people on the outskirts who would like to join or participate that on a rare occasion are let in.    I have performed at Krannert a handful of times, I have spent a lot of time in rehearsal rooms, and I am much more diligent in making choices about which performances I attend.
question404_v2: very little - on the outside, it looks pretty brutalistic... on the inside - it's MCM (not even sure that is the right era) is not super inviting, it's always pretty dark, there usually some students, and the few times I have gone in needing help with something, I have had to wander around since there was no one at the ticket desks.
question405_v2: lighting in the main areas - needs to be better - the darks and the reds may provide ambiance but are not inviting for those with visual stimulation issues.   The mobility accessibility needs to be 100% better than it is. I brought my mom to a performance.  She is not in a wheelchair and does not use a cane, but she is not incredibly agile on her feet. I made sure to choose seating near the handicap accessible seating with few stairs, and access to elevators. It was still a problem.
question406_v2: I have been able to call twice in the past year to get changes to my tickets (change of night, or change from student to adult) and the staff have always been fantastic to work with.

I like ballet, but I'm not going to watch another Nutcracker or Sleeping Beauty.   It needs to be something that while it is familiar it is also still new.

Almost all venues have extremely limited seating for mobility issues. question402_v2: I was born in this area, but my husband and I lived in Phoenix, AZ and I worked for ASU for more than 10 years.  While an employee, I regularly attended  symphony performances, popular music concerts, dance recitals, theater performances, and a wide range of other cultural events and museums.  Upon moving back to this area, I found the opportunities for this kind of cultural enrichment sorely lacking and I missed it greatly.  When I went to work at U of I, I reconnected this is kind of enrichment and actively returned to it.  I much prefer a culturally diverse and rich environment to what is regularly available in the small, outlying community in Central Illinois. It should also be noted that my husband never liked classical music of any kind.  But initial exposure to it in Phoenix plus an on-line course in understanding and appreciating classical music now has him regularly attending the symphony with me.
question404_v2: Any and all of them.  From the CUSO to Moses on the Table to Rodrigo and Gabriela, to all of the artists at the Ellnora Guitar Festival and everything on between.  Oh, and let's not forget the full scale performance of Carmina Burana in 2019!
question405_v2: That's hard for me to answer, as I'm not a member of an underrepresented group.  But I would think that wider advertising of activities that appeal to underrepresented groups would go a long way.  Remember that once you get outside of the immediate area of U of I, the surrounding area is largely white and conservative.  You won't reach a large number of underrepresented groups without reaching out to area all the way out to Peoria and Joliet, Terra Haute, IN and St. Louis.
question406_v2: I've always found the volunteers pleasant and very helpful.  Also the ticket office staff.  I had an instance where we had purchased tickets to a performance by the Chicago Symphony Orchestra.  Our seats were in the balcony and two days before the performance I had a knee go out on me.  I called the next day to find out if there was an elevator to the balcony so that we could attend the performance, but found that there wasn't.  In spite of it being past the deadline for refunds, they gave us a gift card for the price of the tickets, which we used to see the Chicago Symphony the next year.  I offered to simply have them offer the tickets to someone else and didn't ask for a refund, but they gave it to me anyway.  Needless to say, it was a very positive experience.
question408_v2: That's the thing.  I wouldn't ask for anything specific because what's so great about Krannert is that what you offer is so diverse and I'm always finding new things to enjoy.  That's what makes it exciting.

question406_v2: Not really. I don't find the staff, artists, writers, or directors accessible. Given that the Center is part of a University, I'd like to see more explanations, dialogue, and conversations with the audience about the writing and performances. Even the programs have relatively little interesting information in them.
question408_v2: I would like to see a combination of provocative and excellent, vs. plays or concerts that are "safe". For instance, we found Plumas Negras to be safe for 2025. In other words, it reiterated that migrant farmers were abused and underpaid and that generational difficulties existed, which is neither original or new in 2025. But it didn't push me to think and didn't provide me with any moral dilemmas (the bosses were "bad", the main characters were "good"). It didn't provoke me into critical thinking or shift something inside me like good art ought to. Meanwhile, the play was more than 50% in Spanish, so I had to read the screen most of the time rather than looking at the actors' faces. I think this is the part that was supposed to be inclusive and maybe provocative, but it simply took away from the piece. This is just one example.
question502_v2: Pieces that are moving and provocative. Pieces that try to offer a new perspective or make us think. For instance, exploring the issue of reparations or the giving back of land to Native tribes in Illinois, exploring the question of trans folks in athletics, exploring silent addictions that no one ever discusses - like pornography. To me, art should be at the edges of what we are barely talking about - or even a little beyond them.

question405_v2: [I generally do not identify as an underrepresented group, so I understand that my views may not hold as much weight.]  Performances focused towards or representing a specific group offer an opportunity to tell their stories or present certain viewpoints, which can be helpful. However, in a way, they still seem othering. Like, "ok, here's the one performance for your underrepresented group," and most people in the audience might also belong to that group and their viewpoints would be echoed back to them. Rather, to engender a deep-seated sense of belonging and inclusion, more performances could include roles and viewpoints that represent a broader range of people and their experiences. And not to say that Krannert isn't doing this; it seems to be the case in more and more new works. Over time, people will come to expect that performances will have something they can relate to and feel like they belong no matter what they are coming to see.
